speaker: interviewer
text: It's May 3, 1995. And this is an interview with Mrs. Lilly Tykocinski. My name is Brad Kurtzberg. We're in Forest Hills, New York, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English. It's May 3, 1995. I'm here with Mrs. Lilly Tykocinski. My name is Brad Kurtzberg. We're in Forest Hills, New York. And this interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Mrs. Tykocinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Brad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please state and spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Lilly Tykocinski, L-I-L-L-Y T-Y-K-O-C-I-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilly Butnik. And the Hebrew name, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell your last name at birth for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-U-T-N-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Zawiercie, Poland, November 12, 1928. That's the real age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell the name of your hometown for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. Z-A-W-I-E-C-I-E-R. I don't know if I did it right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. And what was that near? Where in Poland was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the closest to Katowice, and not far from Auschwitz, like within 40 kilometers from Katowice, and close to the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your childhood like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a very normal, middle class family. We were seven siblings, four brothers and three sisters. I was the youngest. And we had with us living our grandfather, who stayed with us all the years that I remember. And he passed away in 1938 at the age of 107, officially, they said. And my father, he had butcher business in wholesale and retail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel Butnik. My mother's name was Faigla. And her maiden was Weinreib. My father had one brother and three sisters, and they all had children. My mother had two brothers and one sister. So from the whole family, I survived, my four brothers, and one cousin, that I found after 25 years, and a cousin that she is my sister-in-law now, from people, maybe 60, 70 people in the family. So that's all what we were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a very close family growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close family. Very close family. Listen. In Poland, everybody was a close family. We lived together. We shared the good and the bad together. And everybody respected the parents. Were everything for us. Whatever the father says, goes. We all went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a comfortable home, you know? Not like in the United States comfortable. But for Polish conditions, it was quite comfortable. And we were a happy family. We visited the families. They came to visit us. We shared simchas and all other activities. Tell me a little bit about school growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We happened to have, in Zawiercie, a Jewish school for Jewish children, only Jewish children, which the teachers were also Jewish, and the principal. And this is very unique for Poland, because normally, in every other city, people shared with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of my brothers and sisters attended the school. And I was the last one to attend already that the teachers knew the name already, I was so familiar. So unfortunately, I only finished five grades, because the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the school was very educational, very respectful. The Jewish boys went to cheder and to public school. And the girls had the option to go to Tarbut, was a Hebrew school. This was paid. And the public school was free. And then there was a [YIDDISH], if you wanted to learn Yiddish. I went to [YIDDISH]. My brothers went to cheders to be educated in Jewish. And life was free, comfortable. You were not as free, as Jews, as anybody can think. We knew that we were Jews. On every walk and every street, if we met some Polacks, they were pointing at us that we are Jewish. If there came holidays or Sukkot, and if you make the Sukkos, all the Polacks was screaming the Jews have a holiday. You know, I wasn't completely comfortable with being Jewish, but that was the way of life. Everybody knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish people and how many people, overall, were in your home town.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had 30,000 Polacks and 3,000 Jews. Was a very nice community-- Jewish. We had temples-- synagogues, I mean. Was one big temple. And each section of the city had a synagogue. We lived, as they call it, in the Polish neighborhood. Though we had a synagogue for our people that lived there in the neighborhood, which was very, very beautiful. My father went to the synagogue every day. He prayed every day. We had a kosher home, because everybody was religious, as they say. We didn't drive on Shabbos, because there was nothing to drive, except a bicycle, or winter, ice skates. But everybody was Jewish. We were proud to be Jews and to live there. And we made the best we could. We were happy. Nobody was thinking of leaving the country. To leave Poland was like to desert your family, your lifestyle. And the life went on, till September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we get into the beginning of the war, just tell me a little bit about your father and maybe a special memory you have of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very, very smart man. People, during the war, used to come to ask for advice. He was a good businessman, a good father, and the best husband a woman can have. He adored my mother. I had a very beautiful mom. And he took pride and joy looking at her. He married her because she was beautiful. She was a young teacher. In those days, not everybody went to school. She was 17 years old when he met her. She was teaching. She spoke Polish, Yiddish, and German. And she wrote in all three languages. And that's what my father liked. And he fell in love with her. And he adored my mother. And we saw this respect that he gave her, so we all respected him, and we respected her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom, the best mom. The best mom. But she didn't have time to talk to everybody. Like here, you have to talk to the children. We were seven. Everybody went to school. She was busy raising the family and helping in the business, if it's possible, and doing her chores. But she was a great lady. A great lady. We all loved her dearly. And I lost her very early, which I missed out on a lot. I don't know what it means to say, mom, to ask questions, advice that I needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brothers and sisters? How did you all get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. Each one was an individual for himself, you see, from my oldest to myself. I was the baby, so I was coddled by everybody. I was teased by everybody, loved by everybody, you know? I was given everything that I wanted. We were a very warm family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Tell me a little bit about, maybe, Friday night Shabbos at your home, or a holiday, special kind of an evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always had, for Shabbos, a guest. When my father went to the synagogue, there was always somebody there that was hungry and didn't have where to eat, he was always in my house. My mother was a great cook. And we had full meals. We did not skip on anything. Friday afternoon, that's what I remember. And I was always wondering-- on our street was a house, a friend of mine's. And there were a lot of poor people living without rent, really poor people. And the children of the poor people did not go to school. They came every Friday afternoon with pots to my mother to get a pot of soup and a piece of challah. My mother was giving three, four, five children the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked once-- I don't know, I was maybe eight years old-- I says, how come they don't go to school like I have to go to school? They come here for the soup. I didn't understand why. So my mother explained that they don't have money, and they need to eat. And we have to support them. Every Friday afternoon, they came for the soup with a pot.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was something special. And then they burned down-- during the war, this whole house burned down. I don't know where the people went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war started, you were still a student at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supposed to start school. It was Friday. I'll never forget this day. I went in the morning to the bakery. We didn't buy bread for a day, or two, or three. You needed bread, you went to the bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me to the bakery getting for bread. And I was running. And somebody says the war started. War started. And I says, what's that? War started? So I took the bread, and I was skipping and hopping home-- the war is on, the war is on. I didn't know what I was screaming. For me, it was somehow like an adventure, maybe, because my mother used to tell us about the war, the Prussian and the Austrian that they use to occupy this part of the country. So for me, it looked like fun. And when I came into the house and I says the people said that the war is on-- so my mother says, it's not such a joy. And that's how the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Friday. Saturday morning, we all packed whatever we had. And my brothers got from someplace a horse and a wagon. They put all our belongings-- most of the belongings. And we went with the last bus out to 10 kilometers away from, to another home where my uncle used to live, just around. And my brothers came with the horse with the wagon afterwards. And we stayed there on a Saturday night. Sunday morning, the Germans were there already. We went out on the road. All the children, maybe 10, we went to see what's the noise. So all Germans on the first [INAUDIBLE], the first patrol took already-- they were already in my hometown. They were already, 10 kilometers later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stood them-- they were white-faced, because the roads were full of dust. So you could see just the glasses that they had and the red lips. They didn't do anything to us. We stood there, and we says, the Germans are here. What's next? So we stayed another day with my uncle, wherever there was room to sleep. And then, the next day, we started going back home. I says, if the Germans are here and they were in my house, so what's the use staying here? So we rode back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German marched and they marched, by the thousands. We were going home with the horse and wagon. By the thousands, they were going and taking over Poland, piece by piece, piece by piece. They didn't do, then, anything to us yet, because this was the first troops. And then we arrived back home. And then you started to put your life back together. So there is, right away, no school, no walking out after 8:00. They started taking over the businesses. The Germans made like a Jewish Judenrat. This is a Jewish committee to manage the Jews, to know who was what and where. And there was a beautiful park in Zawiercie, one of the nicest in Poland. There was right away a sign for Jews, no entry. [SPEAKING GERMAN], this means Jews cannot go there. So life started to go down the drain. The food was scarce. They started giving us cards for food rations. And you went to the store, and there was no food. We used to get up 3 o'clock in the morning, stay in the bakery in a line, until 6 o'clock, until they bake the bread, to get a piece of bread. So I stood like from 2:00 till 5:00. 5 o'clock, my brother came to take over, because they were pushing and shoving. There was for everybody, for the Jews and for the Polacks the same, to stay in line to get a loaf of bread. And there were no potatoes. The farmers didn't bring in any food. So my father sent me with a bicycle to the next village to bring a few potatoes home. And life started getting miserable. And then, in 1940, they started taking men to work. So they came to the committee, and they asked for a [NON-ENGLISH] 200, 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what month this was in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but it was in 1940. And they sent you a note to come to this and this place. So one of my brothers had a note. So they were all hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my younger brother says he's going, instead of the other one, because they will not take me. So he went, and they took him. And he was the first one, in 1940, to go to. They are working him, not a concentration camp. That's why he was five years in a working camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, every few months-- they didn't know if you're young, it's good. If you're old, it's good. So my brothers started changing my father's date of birth. He was older, maybe they won't take the older people. And they made him younger and shaved off the beard, to look a little younger. Whatever you did, it wasn't good. They were catching people. They were closing up businesses. My girlfriend had a manufacturing business, materials. They took over the store. They took the men, and they sent them away. And after a few weeks came a little box. And they say they sent, from Auschwitz, the ashes from the men. Nobody believed, because this was just the beginning, like 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father's store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he able to keep operating it? Or what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was operating it. They gave you a [NON-ENGLISH]. This means an amount of meat that you have to distribute to the people with cards. You know, every family gets a pound a week, or a pound a day. So he operated it. Everything was controlled already. If there were big businesses, the Germans went in to control it all. They took it over. But this was food. So he liquidated all the others. He just kept the one that was the closest to the house. And he operated it with my brothers. Then my brothers were taken to camp. Each year, somebody else-- each few months, somebody else was caught to go to concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had mentioned that they closed the schools pretty close to right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They closed our school. There was the one school. And they took over an apartment from a lady, that she lived alone in the five rooms, and they set up classes, from all the kids, from Tarbut and from public school together, with one teacher. And there was a must to learn German. And we were sitting in the class, like for six months, and learning one class. Through the year, I made two classes, sixth and seventh, because this was the most a Jew in public school, seven classes. But we never got the diploma. We never got the graduation. We were sitting just concentrated together, learning not much, because nobody cared any more. We didn't know what we're going to be. And everybody was scared. And then, they felt that the gathering of the children together, it's not healthy, that maybe somebody is doing something against the Germans. So they stopped already the schooling. The schooling stopped completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were at the school, this was the first time you had gone to school with Gentile children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was only Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still only Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even after the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Germans came in. No, we never went to German. We only went to a German school once a week for physics. We didn't have the machinery to make experiments. And the Polish school had a special class. So every Friday, we went for two hours for physics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we came, the Jews are coming. The Jews are coming. This was the familiar chorus. Wanted to get back to the park. You mentioned that they put up a sign saying the Jews couldn't go in the park. Did you used to play in the park a lot before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Every Saturday, I went with my parents, with my sister, to the park. And we had cookies. And there was a little zoo. And the river took place in our city that flowed down. And there was a water that you can go on a little kayak. And a fountain was playing. And an orchestra was playing in the cafe. There was a cafe. It was a beautiful park, very well groomed, beautiful flowers. They had the Polish eagle made out of red flowers and a yellow background. Beautiful. We walked around. You had the weeping willows with benches to sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every Saturday, we went to the park. Every parents took the children to the park. This was a nice place to go, to spend the afternoon. So how did you react when you first saw the sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't react. You're not allowed, you didn't go. They tell you to walk in the gutter, you walked in the gutter, you know? And you have to be home 8 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: 00, if they catch you, they put you to jail. They send you away, or they beat you up. So every parent taught that to their children, be home. Don't go when you're not supposed to go. And we didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the school eventually-- you said it closed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember roughly what year that was, or what time that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably '42. It just went from '40 to '41, one year. And then the school closed. Because '42, we went into the ghetto. I don't remember which month. I really don't remember. I didn't have anybody to ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they first announced the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They closed up parts of the main town with-- like there is a middle road. They closed up this side. The Jews can live in this side. And in the middle, you cannot go. You can go in through here and go through the backyards, from one house to the other house. You cannot go with the main street. You go with the backyards. And they took everybody from around our town, from little towns, from villages, everything went into our ghetto. So we had to leave our house, move as much as we can. They gave us a little house, somebody's, a Polack. They got all the Polacks out from the Jewish neighborhood, and they put all the Jews in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we happened to have a little house with three rooms. So we had rent one room, because we were seven-- we were then already, maybe, four, five people. My brothers were already in the camp, all of them. They were caught in camp. So we went into the ghetto. And in the ghetto, they made one business. My father-- all the people that had businesses, they made one big business. And all of them stayed, and you had to supply the people with food. People were dying from starvation. People didn't have money. People didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: The healthy people, the young people-- there was factory of uniforms, big factory in Zawiercie. And the Germans converted it to make uniforms for the Germans, for the German soldiers. So they took all the young people, and all the able people, and whoever knew somebody, to go to work for the factory. I worked there too. And my father was in the business. My mother was home. My sister-- my older sister had a baby. She was married in the war, in 1942, I think, or '41. She was married. And her husband was caught to camp. And she was pregnant. She had the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she lived with us. We had the three rooms. So in one room, we had to give it to another family. And one room was my sister with the baby. And in the kitchen, we were left, two people and so many families.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was still before we went into the ghetto, when they had constantly announcing or sending you a letter that they need girls, or somebody. So one day, they needed women. And my brother was afraid that we're going to be caught. So he built a double wall. And outdoors, we had like a shack. The dog was there. So he built a double door. And he put pillows in. And we were climbing through the roof into this double-wall. The wall was just that I could stay that way, not to bend and not to move. This way, I could move. And my sister and myself, we stayed there for two nights and two days and watched the Germans. Through a little hole, we peeked, and we saw the Germans coming into our house, looking for somebody to take if-- whoever they will catch, they will take.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there with the rats, with whatever noise there was there, because it was dark and dirty. We stayed there for two days and two nights, hidden, because my brother built this-- this was a cement door, you know, from bricks, and a double wall to hide us. There was always something that you had to escape. I was afraid-- I wasn't ready, you know? I was not, they say, [GERMAN], candidate, because I was young. Because they took like from 14, 15, and I was 12. So if my sister wouldn't be there, they would take me. So I had to hide. Every time something was going on, I had to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting once in a basement by a friend. She was married to a Polack. And he was a doctor. So she put me in the basement in the carpet that was on top. And she locked the outside door, like nobody's home. I was sitting there so scared that I heard like the feet of the Germans were walking over my head. I heard their voices and the stamping of the feet. And they were saying, no Jews. No Jews. No Jews. They were looking around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a few hours, it got quiet. I came out, and I looked. I says the door was locked. How did I hear them? I was so scared that I heard them over my head. That's how it went out, till we went into the ghetto. There was no peace, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all my brother, my brother-in-law, everybody, was already in camp, except one of my brothers. He was working in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made iron, heating iron. And this was exempt from going to concentration camp. If you worked in a special place, you were-- an exemption card.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to just go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one of your brothers was the first one to be taken to a work camp? And can you describe what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mendel. He was the youngest of the boys. My other one was hiding. He went to see what's going on, so he was caught. And they kept them together in the school, in our school. That's why they closed the school. They needed the school to assemble people. And he was kept there. And he was explaining that he is not the one, that my other brother is not here. There was no way out. He was gone. He went to camp the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, Bunzlau. Bunzlau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he was there, were you able to communicate with him at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We sent him packages. He wrote us letters. And we wrote to him. That's why this letter went to him, to the camp. Not to this one. He was in two or three different ones. But I think Bunzlau, or [NON-ENGLISH] was the first one. I'm not sure, you know? And then, my other brother was caught. And they met together in the same camp. All three brothers-- my oldest, second oldest, and the youngest-- were together almost in all the three camps. Except the third one stayed home with us. And he was working in this iron factory. He was exempt from going to camp. He was once, and then we got him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, there was so much going on that you-- if you had papers, and if you had somebody that you know that they need you, you could have managed to come back. But for how long? Nobody knew how long this coming back will be. OK.
